title: Europe's Chip Squeeze, China's Lithography Gap, and the New Orbital Reconstitution Race
subhead: An EU-funded report calls Europe's semiconductor future 'bleak' between Beijing's export controls and Washington's tech leverage. China's EUV prototype gets a sober reckoning. DARPA asks industry how to rebuild a satellite constellation in hours, not years. The G7 sets a 60% rare earth dependency ceiling for 2030.

sections.heading: Europe's Chip Sector Called 'Bleak' Between China and US Pressures
sections.body:
An EU-funded report from the EU Institute for Security Studies (EUISS) and French think tank Institut Montaigne, published Thursday July 2, warns that Europe's semiconductor industry faces a 'bleak future' squeezed between Chinese export controls on critical minerals and deepening dependence on US design software and manufacturing tools. Reuters' Toby Sterling reported the findings from Amsterdam.

The report, part of an 18-month Chips Diplomacy Support Initiative, identifies two asymmetrical threats. China's leverage is blunt and immediate — deployable within weeks through export licensing on rare earths and materials that Europe cannot easily source elsewhere. Washington's leverage is slower but more corrosive, embedded in decades of dependence on American EDA software and process technology. EUISS policy analyst Joris Teer told Reuters that 'dependence on Washington seems to have become of much greater concern under the second Trump administration.'

The report flags the MATCH Act, a bill moving through Congress that would let Washington impose export controls unilaterally on allies that do not align their China policy within a set window. That is acutely relevant for the Netherlands' ASML — now Europe's most valuable listed company — whose lithography exports to China remain a recurring transatlantic flashpoint. Teer's prescription is narrower than the threat: Europe's 'only viable path' is to build on strengths it already has, naming ASML specifically, rather than chasing broad self-sufficiency. That framing aligns with the EU's Chips Act 2.0 proposal, revealed in June, which shifted from the original Chips Act's abandoned 20% global market share target to demand-side incentives. The report lands weeks after the EU formally joined Pax Silica, a US-led chip pact that France publicly criticized as 'technological colonisation.'
sections.whyItMatters: Europe is confronting a dual-dependency trap it can neither quickly escape nor easily manage: immediate Chinese mineral leverage on one side and structural American technology lock-in on the other, with no consensus in Brussels on which threat is more urgent.

sections.heading: China's EUV Lithography: Parsing Signal From Noise
sections.body:
A detailed analysis in The Diplomat, published July 2, provides a sober framework for assessing China's EUV lithography progress after the December 2025 Reuters report that researchers in Shenzhen had secretly built an EUV prototype. The piece identifies three specific technical bottlenecks where China lags behind ASML's capabilities: EUV light sources (China: 100-150 watts vs. industry standard 250-600 watts), mirror reflectance rates (China: 65% vs. Zeiss: 70%), and photoresists (China cannot produce viable EUV photoresists at the required sub-parts-per-billion purity levels).

The analysis highlights that China's breakthrough in laser-produced plasma in March 2025 came from a team at the Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics led by Lin Nan, who previously led light source technology at ASML — underscoring how much of China's progress relies on talent poaching and hybrid engineering (reverse-engineering Western technology combined with domestic innovation) rather than pure indigenous development. Analyst Greg Allen has argued China's EUV prototype was more likely the result of evading export controls than genuine domestic innovation, and TechInsights vice chair Dan Hutcheson noted that 'the harder they run, they just stay in place' — because ASML continues advancing.

Chinese insiders claim 2030 is a 'realistic target' for making working chips from the prototype, while skeptics posit decades to commercial viability. The Diplomat's framework offers a way to cut through this range: track progress on the three specific bottlenecks rather than treating the EUV question as monolithic.
sections.whyItMatters: The EUV question determines whether China can eventually circumvent the entire architecture of US-led chip export controls. The Diplomat's bottleneck framework gives policymakers a concrete monitoring tool — but the talent poaching and export control evasion angles suggest the contest is as much about enforcement as about technology.

sections.heading: G7 Draws a 60% Rare Earth Dependency Red Line for 2030
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Évian, France, leaders agreed to reduce dependence on any single non-G7 supplier for rare earths and permanent magnets to under 60% by 2030, with a longer-term ambition of 50%. The declaration does not name China directly, but the target is clearly aimed at Beijing's current dominance: the IEA estimates China accounted for roughly 91% of global production in separation and refining of magnet rare earths in 2024, and about 94% of permanent magnet production.

The G7 pointed to 195 projects announced since the start of 2026, representing about $74 billion in investment across critical mineral value chains — mining, processing, recycling, and manufacturing in G7 and partner countries. Ministers were tasked with setting specific dependency targets for other critical minerals before year-end.

The backdrop is China's April 2025 export controls on medium and heavy rare earths — samarium, terbium, dysprosium, and certain permanent magnet materials — which the IEA said caused export volumes to fall sharply, leaving some carmakers in the US and Europe struggling to obtain permanent magnets, with some manufacturers forced to cut utilization rates or temporarily shut down factories. China's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lin Jian rejected the characterization, saying Beijing's export controls are 'in line with international practice' and accusing the G7 of using 'small cliques' that undermine the international economic and trade order.

Japan's experience looms large: after a 2010 maritime dispute, Tokyo spent years reducing its dependence on Chinese rare earths — a process that demonstrated how hard diversification actually is. China recently tightened controls on dual-use exports to Japan after remarks by Japan's leader about Taiwan.
sections.whyItMatters: The 60% target is the first time the G7 has set a quantified dependency ceiling for rare earths — but the gap between political targets and operational supply chains remains vast, and China has already demonstrated its willingness to weaponize these supply lines.

sections.heading: DARPA Asks Industry: Can You Rebuild a Satellite Constellation in Hours?
sections.body:
DARPA has issued a request for information asking industry how quickly the US could restore critical space services after a satellite constellation is destroyed, jammed, hacked, or blinded — with a target of hours to weeks, not months or years. The RFI, reported by Voz Populi, signals that defense planners no longer treat satellites as untouchable assets but as first-day targets in a potential conflict.

The 2026 US intelligence threat assessment identifies space as a 'central arena for strategic competition,' with China as the key competitor. The Space Force says China conducted 93 space launches in 2025 and ended the year with over 1,353 satellites in orbit, including more than 510 ISR-capable satellites. DARPA's request goes beyond simply keeping spares: it asks for ideas on modular spacecraft, software-defined payloads, rapid manufacturing, fast launch integration, distributed constellations, and on-orbit assembly.

The urgency is grounded in recent demonstrations. The Space Force's Victus Haze mission on June 19, 2026 saw a Rocket Lab Electron launch just 16 hours and 42 minutes after receiving the notice to launch — beating the earlier Victus Nox benchmark by more than 10 hours. But fast launch only matters if there is something ready to launch. A satellite that takes three years to build cannot help in a three-day crisis.

DARPA's framing also carries a deterrence dimension: if the US can show that lost space services can return quickly, an adversary may decide that attacking them is less useful than expected.
sections.whyItMatters: The RFI reveals how seriously the Pentagon now takes the possibility of a first-day attack on US space assets in a Pacific conflict — and how far current reconstitution capability is from the resilience DARPA wants to build.

sections.heading: FCC Moves to Slash Satellite Licensing Timelines; Amazon Leo Reaches 396 Satellites
sections.body:
The FCC announced it will vote on July 22 on a Space Modernization Order that aims to cut satellite licensing timelines from over a year to weeks or months. The order creates a 'licensing assembly line' with a presumption that qualifying applications serve the public interest if they meet commission rules. The move follows earlier FCC action updating decades-old satellite spectrum-sharing rules that could give Starlink a capacity boost of up to sevenfold.

Meanwhile, United Launch Alliance's Atlas V 551 flew its final satellite mission on July 2, carrying 29 Amazon Leo broadband satellites to orbit. The rocket — powered by the Russian-built RD-180 engine, now retired because Congress restricted further military launch contracts on RD-180-powered vehicles after Russia's 2014 annexation of Crimea — closes a 20-year chapter in US heavy-lift spaceflight. Amazon Leo's constellation now stands at 396 active satellites, well short of the FCC's originally mandated 1,618-satellite interim deadline (which the FCC waived on June 5). Full deployment of 3,236 satellites remains due by July 30, 2029.

Amazon's next deployment phase depends on two providers — ULA's Vulcan Centaur (38 launches purchased) and Blue Origin's New Glenn (27 launches) — that are both currently grounded. The company has used Atlas V, SpaceX's Falcon 9, and Arianespace's Ariane 6 so far, structuring its portfolio to avoid single-vehicle dependency. But the gap between 396 and 3,236 satellites will test whether that diversification strategy holds under real-world launch constraints.
sections.whyItMatters: The FCC is trying to remove regulatory friction from the satellite broadband race just as it intensifies — Starlink vs. Amazon Leo vs. others. But Amazon's deployment math shows how quickly even well-capitalized players can fall behind schedule when next-generation launch vehicles are not yet operational.

sections.heading: MUFG: US Controls 75% of Global Frontier AI Compute; Taiwan Remains the Silicon Chokepoint
sections.body:
A 107-page MUFG report titled 'Bottlenecks to Scaling AI Computational Power' projects that the US will invest $2 trillion in AI infrastructure, software, and services in 2026 — nearly 20 times India's projected $95 billion. China is expected to invest $355 billion, followed by the UK ($205 billion), Germany ($180 billion), Japan ($155 billion), and France ($110 billion). The US currently controls around 75% of global frontier AI computing power, while China accounts for about 15%, leaving the rest of the world with a small share.

The report identifies 10 critical bottlenecks to AI scaling, including semiconductor manufacturing, memory chips, electricity, data centers, strategic minerals, export controls, and geopolitical risks around Taiwan. It estimates that TSMC manufactures roughly 90% of the world's advanced AI chips, calling Taiwan the world's most important 'silicon chokepoint' and warning that supply-chain diversification is progressing more slowly than geopolitical risks require.

Perhaps the report's most striking claim is that electricity, not processors, could become the defining bottleneck for AI expansion. It projects that capex by the world's five largest hyperscalers — Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Oracle — will reach $775 billion in 2026 before crossing $1 trillion in 2027. Meta's planned Hyperion data center in Louisiana, for instance, would span 2,250 acres and consume roughly 5 GW of power — equivalent to a mid-sized American city.
sections.whyItMatters: The compute concentration is staggering: three-quarters of the world's frontier AI capacity sits in one country, and nine-tenths of the most advanced chips come from one island. The MUFG report underscores that the next AI competition is about physical infrastructure — power plants, data centers, and chip fabs — not just algorithms.

sections.heading: Türkiye Angle: Baykar-Piaggio Anniversary and the Drone Power Shift in Europe
sections.body:
Baykar and Piaggio Aerospace marked the first anniversary of their collaboration this week, following Baykar's acquisition of the 140-year-old Italian aircraft manufacturer, finalized June 30, 2025. The newly formed Baykar Piaggio Aerospace SpA has retained over 675 employees and set a production target of 20-25 aircraft annually, with plans to develop the Avanti NX and a military variant called the Mantide — both built around hybrid propulsion and autonomous operations.

The anniversary coincides with a broader shift in European defense thinking. Bloomberg reported that as the US reduces its military commitment to the continent, Turkey is positioning itself to benefit both geopolitically and economically. Erdoğan's government is leveraging Turkey's drone warfare expertise — Baykar's TB2 and Akinci platforms are now deployed across multiple conflict zones — as a form of strategic currency within NATO. Meanwhile, the drone arms race continues to accelerate globally, with Aviation Today documenting the rapid proliferation of loitering munitions and autonomous systems across multiple theaters.

The combination is significant: Turkey is no longer just a drone exporter. Through Piaggio, Baykar now has a European manufacturing base, a 140-year Italian aviation brand, and direct access to EU industrial standards — turning Turkey's defense technology push into a European industrial presence.
sections.whyItMatters: Baykar's acquisition of Piaggio turns Turkey's drone export success into a European industrial foothold, giving Ankara both a manufacturing presence inside the EU and a platform for next-generation aircraft development at a time when European defense procurement is being rethought from scratch.

bottomLine: This week's tech-power developments circle a common theme: the gap between political targets and operational reality. The G7 set a 60% rare earth dependency ceiling for 2030, but China already controls 91-94% of key processing stages and has demonstrated willingness to weaponize that control. The EU's own think tank called Europe's chip future 'bleak' between Beijing's export controls and Washington's technology lock-in, with no consensus on which threat is more urgent. DARPA is now asking industry how to rebuild satellite constellations in hours — revealing how far current resilience is from what a Pacific conflict would demand. China's EUV prototype is real but still years from viable production, hampered by bottlenecks in light sources, mirrors, and photoresists. And the MUFG report confirmed that 75% of the world's frontier AI compute and 90% of its most advanced chips sit in two dangerously concentrated geographies. The infrastructure race is underway — the question is whether supply chains and policy can move fast enough to matter.
sources: Reuters, The Diplomat, Voz Populi, The Next Web, TechTimes, Benzinga, PCMag, Fortune India, MUFG, EUISS/Institut Montaigne, Space Force, DARPA, IEA, RaillyNews, Aviation Today, Bloomberg, China Daily, Spaceflight Now
